source.title: Pain: A Precision Signal for Reinforcement Learning and Control

confidence_rationale: Authoritative computational review (Neuron) recasting pain as a precision-weighted RL signal with dual function: sensory precision for learning and motivational signal for control. This dual-function framing maps well to z_harm_s (precise, forward-predictable, used for attribution learning) vs z_harm_a (motivational, drives behavioral control via E3). The precision-weighting framework explains why prediction can attenuate z_harm_s (precision cancellation) while z_harm_a remains as the motivational drive. However, Seymour does not explicitly formalize two separate streams — the paper treats pain as a single signal with multiple computational roles.
failure_signatures: Seymour explicitly frames pain as a single integrated signal, not two separable streams. The dual-function interpretation is REE's mapping, not the paper's claim.
The RL framework may overemphasize motivational function at the expense of the sensory-discriminative pathway's independent computational role.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The core function of pain is motivational -- to direct both short- and long-term behavior away from harm. A reinforcement learning model of pain explains why pain is tuned by multiple factors and necessarily supported by a distributed network of brain regions, recasting pain as a precise and objectifiable control signal. Pain is conceptualized as the internal reinforcement signal used for learning and is distinct from the nociceptive sensing process.
mapping.ree_translation: Seymour's distinction between nociceptive sensing (detection) and pain (motivational RL signal) maps onto z_harm_s (nociceptive sensing: precise, forward-predictable, used by E2_harm_s forward model for attribution) vs z_harm_a (pain as motivational signal: drives E3 behavioral control, scales urgency for commit gating). The precision-weighting framework explains SD-011's key asymmetry: z_harm_s can be attenuated by prediction (precision cancellation when forward model accurately predicts harm) while z_harm_a cannot be cancelled because it IS the control signal that must remain operative regardless of prediction accuracy.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Seymour treats pain as a single precision-weighted signal, not two anatomically separable streams. The mapping to SD-011's dual-stream architecture requires interpreting his sensing/motivation distinction as implying separable computational pathways, which goes beyond the paper's explicit claims. The paper does not address spinal cord or brainstem dual-pathway anatomy (Adelta vs C-fiber).
mapping.source_context: Computational review in Neuron. Integrates reinforcement learning, active inference, optimal control, and information theory. Based on human behavioral and neuroimaging studies of pain avoidance learning.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Neuron, Seymour is a leading computational pain researcher). Mapping fidelity moderate: the sensing/motivation distinction supports SD-011's rationale but the paper does not formalize two separate streams. Transfer risk low: computational framework directly about pain processing architecture.
